title: 10.2 Energy Flow
description: Trophic levels, energy pyramids, and productivity
expanded_description: An introduction to how energy moves through ecosystems—from the sun to producers to consumers and eventually to decomposers. This guide explains trophic levels (feeding positions in a food chain), energy pyramids (graphical representations of energy loss), and productivity (the rate at which energy is captured and stored). It covers why only about 10% of energy passes from one level to the next, how this limits food chain length, and how human activities affect energy flow in ecosystems.

core_concept.principle: Energy flows through ecosystems in one direction: from the sun (or chemical sources) to producers, then to consumers, and finally to decomposers. At each transfer, about 90% of the energy is lost as heat (used for metabolism, movement, growth), leaving only about 10% to be passed to the next trophic level. This 10% rule explains why food chains are short (usually 4-5 levels) and why there are far fewer top predators than producers. The rate of energy capture by producers is called primary productivity, and it sets the upper limit for energy available to the rest of the ecosystem.
core_concept.explanation:
Every living thing needs energy to grow, move, reproduce, and maintain itself. The ultimate source of energy for almost all ecosystems is the sun. Plants, algae, and some bacteria capture this solar energy through photosynthesis and convert it into chemical energy (glucose). This chemical energy then flows through the ecosystem as organisms eat each other.

Trophic levels:
• Level 1: Producers (autotrophs)—Plants, algae, cyanobacteria. They capture energy from the sun (or chemical energy in deep-sea vents) and make their own food.
• Level 2: Primary consumers (herbivores)—Animals that eat plants (rabbits, grasshoppers, deer).
• Level 3: Secondary consumers (carnivores)—Animals that eat herbivores (snakes, foxes, small fish).
• Level 4: Tertiary consumers (top predators)—Animals that eat other carnivores (eagles, wolves, sharks).
• Decomposers—Break down dead matter at all levels, returning nutrients to the soil.

Why so much energy is lost:
At each trophic level, energy is used for:
• Metabolism: Breaking down food, moving, maintaining body temperature (heat loss).
• Growth and reproduction: Only a small fraction of the energy eaten becomes new biomass.
• Not eaten: Some parts of organisms (bones, hair, shells) aren't consumed.
• Waste: Energy is lost in feces and urine.

This is why only about 10% of the energy from one level is available to the next. If a field of grass captures 10,000 units of energy from the sun, rabbits eating the grass get about 1,000 units. Foxes eating the rabbits get about 100 units. Eagles eating the foxes get about 10 units.

Productivity:
• Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total energy captured by producers through photosynthesis.
• Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Energy remaining after producers use some for their own metabolism. NPP = GPP - respiration. NPP is the energy available to consumers.
• Secondary Productivity: Rate of energy storage by consumers (growth, reproduction).

Fun fact: The most productive ecosystems on Earth are tropical rainforests (high NPP) and coral reefs. The least productive are deserts and the deep ocean. About 50% of Earth's primary productivity occurs in the oceans, mostly from microscopic algae (phytoplankton).

image.alt: A diagram showing energy flow: sun → producers (grass, trees) → primary consumers (rabbit, deer) → secondary consumers (fox, snake) → tertiary consumers (eagle, wolf) → decomposers (mushrooms, bacteria). Arrows show energy moving one way. An energy pyramid shows the 10% rule: producers (10,000 units), primary consumers (1,000 units), secondary consumers (100 units), tertiary consumers (10 units).
image.caption: Energy flow through an ecosystem: Energy enters from the sun, is captured by producers, and moves up trophic levels. Only about 10% of energy passes to the next level—the rest is lost as heat.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Flow of Energy in Nature
sections.introduction:
Look at any ecosystem—a forest, a pond, a grassland—and you'll see a complex web of life. But behind all the interactions is a simple, powerful principle: energy flows. It enters as sunlight, is captured by plants, and passes from one organism to another as they eat and are eaten. Eventually, all the energy that entered the ecosystem leaves as heat.

Unlike nutrients (which cycle), energy does not recycle. Once energy is used, it's gone. That's why ecosystems need a constant input of energy—mostly from the sun. If the sun stopped shining, life on Earth would soon end.

Key ideas in energy flow:
• Trophic levels: The "feeding levels" in a food chain (producers, herbivores, carnivores).
• Energy pyramids: Graphical representations showing energy decreasing at each level.
• The 10% rule: Only about 10% of energy passes from one level to the next.
• Productivity: How fast energy is captured and stored (like the "interest" on the energy bank account).

Understanding energy flow is essential for environmental science. It explains why there are so few top predators, why food chains are short, and why human activities that affect productivity (deforestation, overfishing) have cascading effects.

It also has practical implications. When we eat meat, we are eating at a higher trophic level than when we eat plants. Because of the 10% rule, a plant-based diet requires far less land and resources than a meat-based diet. A field of grain can feed many more people directly than if the grain is fed to animals first.

In this section, we'll explore how energy flows through ecosystems, how we measure it, and why it matters for conservation and sustainability.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing the sun shining on a forest. Arrows show energy entering the forest, moving through plants, animals, and decomposers, and leaving as heat. A comparison: plant-based meal (direct energy) vs. meat-based meal (energy lost at each step).
introduction_image.caption: Energy flows one way through ecosystems. Plant-based diets are more energy-efficient because they skip the energy loss that happens when feeding plants to animals.

key_concept.definition: Energy flows through ecosystems in a one-way stream: from the sun to producers (autotrophs) to consumers (heterotrophs) to decomposers, and finally out as heat. Trophic levels represent steps in this flow. Only about 10% of energy transfers from one level to the next—the rest is lost as heat, used for metabolism, or not consumed. This limits the number of trophic levels and the biomass of top predators.
key_concept.context:
This pattern is universal across all ecosystems, whether a tropical rainforest or a desert. The specifics vary—different ecosystems have different productivity levels—but the shape is always the same: a wide base of producers, narrowing through consumers, to a tiny top of apex predators.

Key patterns in energy flow:
• Energy pyramids are always upright: There must be more energy (and usually more biomass) at lower trophic levels. You can't have more wolves than rabbits.
• Food chains are short: Typically 4-5 levels. After that, there's not enough energy to support another level.
• Biomass decreases up the pyramid: The total mass of organisms decreases at higher levels.

Productivity varies dramatically across ecosystems:
• High productivity: Tropical rainforests, coral reefs, estuaries, wetlands. These ecosystems support high biodiversity and abundant life.
• Low productivity: Deserts, deep ocean, arctic tundra. These have sparse life and slow growth.

Real-world connection: Overfishing removes top predators (tuna, sharks) from marine ecosystems. This can cause trophic cascades—populations of smaller fish explode, which overgraze plankton, which reduces overall productivity. Understanding energy flow helps predict these effects.

image.alt: A food chain diagram with trophic levels: Producers (grass) → Primary consumers (grasshopper) → Secondary consumers (frog) → Tertiary consumers (snake) → Quaternary consumers (hawk). An energy pyramid next to it showing decreasing energy at each level.
image.caption: Trophic levels: Energy moves from producers up through consumers. At each step, about 90% of the energy is lost, leaving only 10% for the next level.

sections.key_concept: Trophic levels organize feeding relationships. Energy pyramids show the decrease in energy, biomass, and numbers at higher levels. Productivity measures the rate of energy capture (primary productivity) and energy storage (secondary productivity).

classifications.value: Understanding the different positions in food chains and webs, and the roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers.
characteristics: Producers (Trophic Level 1): Autotrophs that make their own food. Photoautotrophs: plants, algae, cyanobacteria (use sunlight). Chemoautotrophs: bacteria at hydrothermal vents (use chemical energy). Producers are the foundation of all ecosystems—without them, there would be no energy for other organisms.
Primary Consumers (Trophic Level 2): Herbivores that eat producers. Examples: rabbits, deer, grasshoppers, zooplankton (tiny animals in oceans). They convert plant material into animal tissue.
Secondary Consumers (Trophic Level 3): Carnivores that eat primary consumers. Examples: snakes, foxes, small fish, spiders. Some are omnivores (eat both plants and animals).
Tertiary Consumers (Trophic Level 4): Carnivores that eat secondary consumers. Examples: eagles, wolves, sharks, large fish. These are often top predators in their ecosystems.

Apex Predators (Top Level): Predators with no natural predators. Examples: lions, orcas, great white sharks, humans (in many ecosystems). They play critical roles in regulating populations of lower levels.

Decomposers (All Levels): Bacteria, fungi, and detritivores (earthworms, beetles) that break down dead matter. They are not typically assigned a trophic level because they feed on waste from all levels. They are essential for nutrient cycling.
classifications.behavior:
Trophic levels are not always clear-cut. Many animals feed at multiple levels. A bear eats berries (primary consumer), fish (secondary or tertiary consumer), and occasionally a deer (tertiary consumer). Omnivores and opportunistic feeders can shift levels depending on food availability.

Top predators often control the populations of lower levels. When wolves were removed from Yellowstone, elk populations exploded and overgrazed willows. When wolves were reintroduced, elk behavior changed (they avoided stream areas), and willows recovered. This is called a trophic cascade—the effect of a top predator rippling down through lower levels.

Decomposers are often overlooked but are absolutely critical. In forests, fungi break down fallen trees; bacteria decompose dead animals. Without decomposers, nutrients would be locked in dead matter, and new plants couldn't grow. The forest would literally be buried in its own dead wood.

Real-world connection: Overfishing of sharks (apex predators) has caused trophic cascades in marine ecosystems. With fewer sharks, rays and smaller sharks increase. They eat more scallops and clams, causing collapse of shellfish fisheries. This shows how removing top predators can have unexpected economic consequences.

examples.explanation:
The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995 created a classic trophic cascade: (1) Before wolves: Wolves were eliminated in the early 1900s. Elk populations grew unchecked. Elk overgrazed willows and aspens along streams. (2) Effects of overgrazing: Without willows, beavers had no food or building material; beaver populations declined. Without beaver dams, streams eroded and wetlands dried up. Songbirds lost habitat. (3) Wolves reintroduced: Wolves reduced elk numbers and changed elk behavior—elk avoided stream areas where they could be ambushed. (4) Ecosystem recovery: Willows and aspens regrew. Beavers returned, building dams that restored wetlands. Songbird populations increased. (5) Evidence: Studies showed that streamside vegetation increased dramatically after wolf reintroduction, even though elk populations didn't drop dramatically—behavior change (elk staying away from risky areas) was the key.

Why this matters: This shows that energy flow isn't just about numbers—it's about interactions. Top predators shape ecosystems not just by eating prey, but by changing prey behavior. Removing predators can have cascading effects that ripple through entire food webs.

image.alt: A before-and-after diagram: Before wolves (elk overgrazing willows, eroded stream, no beavers). After wolves reintroduced (elk avoid stream, willows regrow, beaver dam, restored wetland). Photos of Yellowstone streams before and after.
image.caption: Trophic cascade in Yellowstone: Wolves changed elk behavior, allowing willows to regrow, beavers to return, and wetlands to recover.

classifications.value: Understanding the graphical representation of energy, biomass, and numbers at each trophic level, and why pyramids are always upright.
characteristics: Pyramid of Energy: Shows the amount of energy available at each trophic level per unit area per year (e.g., kcal/m²/year). Always upright (wide base, narrow top) because energy decreases at each level. Energy loss is about 90% per level, so the pyramid is very steep.

Pyramid of Biomass: Shows the total mass of organisms at each trophic level (e.g., grams/m²). Usually upright, but can be inverted in some aquatic ecosystems (zooplankton biomass can exceed phytoplankton biomass because phytoplankton reproduce very quickly).

Pyramid of Numbers: Shows the number of individual organisms at each trophic level. Often upright, but can be inverted (one tree can support thousands of insects, so numbers of producers may be less than numbers of primary consumers).
Energy units: Typically measured in kilocalories (kcal) or joules. One kcal = amount of energy needed to raise 1 kg of water by 1°C. This allows comparison across different ecosystems.

Ecological efficiency: The percentage of energy transferred from one trophic level to the next. Typically 5-20%, average about 10%. Affected by: (1) consumption efficiency (how much is eaten), (2) assimilation efficiency (how much is digested), (3) production efficiency (how much becomes new biomass).
classifications.behavior:
The pyramid of energy is the most fundamental—it can never be inverted because of the laws of thermodynamics. Energy is always lost as heat at each step. This is why there can never be more energy at a higher trophic level than at a lower one.

Biomass pyramids can sometimes be inverted. In the open ocean, phytoplankton (producers) have very low biomass at any given moment, but they reproduce rapidly (multiple times per day). Zooplankton (primary consumers) can have higher biomass because they live longer. But the energy pyramid is still upright—the phytoplankton are producing energy so fast that their total energy output over time exceeds that of the zooplankton.

Numbers pyramids can also be inverted. A single oak tree (producer) supports thousands of insects (primary consumers). The tree has low numbers but high biomass. The insects have high numbers but low biomass. The energy pyramid remains upright.

Real-world connection: Understanding energy pyramids helps explain why fish farming is energy-intensive. Farmed salmon are tertiary consumers—they eat fish meal made from smaller fish (secondary consumers). To produce 1 kg of salmon, you need about 5-10 kg of wild fish. This is inefficient compared to farming plants (producers) or even herbivorous fish (like tilapia).

examples.explanation:
The open ocean has a unique energy pyramid: (1) Producers: Phytoplankton—microscopic algae. They have low biomass at any moment (they are eaten quickly), but extremely high productivity (they reproduce rapidly). (2) Primary consumers: Zooplankton—tiny animals that eat phytoplankton. Their biomass can be greater than phytoplankton biomass (inverted biomass pyramid). (3) Secondary consumers: Small fish (herring, sardines) that eat zooplankton. (4) Tertiary consumers: Larger fish (tuna, salmon) that eat small fish. (5) Top predators: Sharks, whales, humans.

The energy pyramid is upright even though the biomass pyramid is inverted. The phytoplankton produce so much energy per day (high turnover) that they support all the consumers above them, even though their standing biomass is small.

Why this matters: This shows that productivity (rate of energy capture) is more important than standing biomass. The ocean is highly productive even though it looks "empty"—most of the life is microscopic and invisible to us.

image.alt: An energy pyramid: phytoplankton (high energy), zooplankton, small fish, large fish, top predators. A biomass pyramid showing phytoplankton biomass low but productivity high (arrows showing rapid turnover). Microscopic images of phytoplankton and zooplankton.
image.caption: Ocean energy pyramid: Phytoplankton have low standing biomass but high productivity (rapid reproduction). This supports the entire marine food web.

classifications.type: Productivity: How Fast Energy Is Captured
classifications.value: Understanding primary productivity (energy capture by producers) and secondary productivity (energy storage by consumers), and how they vary across ecosystems.
characteristics: Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total energy captured by producers through photosynthesis (or chemosynthesis). Measured in energy per area per time (e.g., kcal/m²/year). Includes energy used by producers for their own metabolism.

Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Energy remaining after producers use some for respiration. NPP = GPP - respiration. NPP is the energy available to consumers. NPP determines how much life an ecosystem can support.

Factors affecting NPP: Sunlight, water, temperature, nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus), CO₂. Highest NPP where these are abundant (tropical rainforests, coral reefs, wetlands). Lowest NPP where they are scarce (deserts, deep ocean, polar regions).

Global NPP: Total NPP of Earth is about 100-120 billion tons of carbon per year. About 50% from oceans (mostly phytoplankton), 50% from land. Humans appropriate about 25% of global NPP for food, fiber, and fuel—leaving less for other species.

Secondary Productivity: Rate of energy storage by consumers (growth, reproduction). Varies with food quality, temperature, and species. Endotherms (birds, mammals) have lower production efficiency (1-3%) than ectotherms (fish, reptiles, insects) (10-30%) because they use more energy for maintaining body temperature.
classifications.behavior:
NPP is like the "interest" on the Earth's energy bank account. It's the amount of new energy available each year for consumers. Ecosystems with high NPP (rainforests, coral reefs) support high biodiversity. Ecosystems with low NPP (deserts) support few species.

NPP varies dramatically across Earth:
• Highest: Tropical rainforest (2000-3000 g/m²/year), coral reefs (2500 g/m²/year), estuaries (2000 g/m²/year).
• Moderate: Temperate forests (1000 g/m²/year), grasslands (600 g/m²/year), croplands (300-1000 g/m²/year depending on inputs).
• Lowest: Deserts (100 g/m²/year), tundra (100-200 g/m²/year), open ocean (100-150 g/m²/year).

Human activities affect NPP dramatically:
• Deforestation: Reduces NPP by removing trees.
• Agriculture: Replaces natural vegetation with crops; NPP of croplands is often lower than original forest but can be maintained with fertilizers.
• Fertilizer runoff: Increases NPP in lakes and coastal oceans, causing algal blooms (eutrophication).
• Climate change: Shifts NPP patterns; some areas become more productive, others less.

Real-world connection: The global human appropriation of NPP (about 25%) means that one-quarter of the energy that would otherwise support wild species is taken for human use. This is a major driver of biodiversity loss—there's less energy left for other species.

examples.process: Eutrophication: When Too Much Productivity Is Bad
examples.explanation:
Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients (especially nitrogen and phosphorus) cause explosive growth of algae: (1) Sources: Fertilizer runoff from farms, sewage, detergents. (2) Algal bloom: Algae grow rapidly, covering the water surface. This blocks sunlight from reaching underwater plants. (3) Death and decomposition: When algae die, decomposers (bacteria) consume them. The bacteria use up oxygen in the water (hypoxia). (4) Dead zone: Oxygen levels drop so low that fish and other aquatic animals suffocate. (5) Example: The Gulf of Mexico dead zone (up to 20,000 km²) forms every summer from nutrient runoff from the Mississippi River. (6) Solutions: Reduce fertilizer use, buffer strips (plants that absorb runoff), better sewage treatment.

Why this matters: Eutrophication shows that productivity can be too high. The problem isn't too much energy—it's too much nutrient input, which disrupts the natural balance. The algal bloom temporarily increases productivity, but the crash creates a dead zone where productivity falls to zero.

image.alt: A diagram of eutrophication: Fertilizer runoff → algal bloom → algae die → bacteria consume oxygen → dead zone. A map of the Gulf of Mexico dead zone. A photo of green algae covering a lake.
image.caption: Eutrophication: Excess nutrients cause algal blooms. When algae die, decomposers use up oxygen, creating dead zones where fish can't survive.

principles.principle: The 10% Rule Limits Food Chain Length
principles.statement: Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next level. The other 90% is lost as heat, used for metabolism, or not consumed. After 4-5 transfers, so little energy remains that another trophic level cannot be supported.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Food chains are rarely more than 5 levels long.
• There are far more producers than top predators.
• Plant-based diets are more energy-efficient than meat-based diets.
• Predators need large territories (they need enough prey to survive).

Everyday example: If a farmer grows corn, the energy in that corn can feed many people directly. If the farmer feeds the corn to cows, then eats the cows, only 10% of the corn's energy reaches the people. That's why meat is more expensive—it takes more resources to produce.
principles.example: A 1,000 kg field of grass (producer) supports 100 kg of rabbits (primary consumers). Those rabbits support 10 kg of foxes (secondary consumers). Those foxes support 1 kg of eagles (tertiary consumers). If you wanted to add another level (eagles eaten by something), you would need 0.1 kg of that predator—barely enough for one small animal. This is why you rarely see food chains with more than 4-5 levels.
principles.analogy: Energy is like money in a bank account. At each trophic level, you spend 90% of the money on bills (metabolism). Only 10% goes into savings (new biomass). After a few steps, the account is empty.

image.alt: An energy pyramid with numbers: 10,000 kcal producers, 1,000 kcal primary consumers, 100 kcal secondary consumers, 10 kcal tertiary consumers. A diagram showing energy loss as heat at each level.
image.caption: The 10% rule: Only 10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next. This limits food chain length and explains why there are far more producers than top predators.

principles.statement: The net primary productivity (NPP) of an ecosystem sets the upper limit on the energy available to consumers. Higher NPP ecosystems can support more individuals, more species, and larger top predators. Low NPP ecosystems have sparse life and are more vulnerable to disturbance.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Tropical rainforests have the highest biodiversity.
• Deserts have low biodiversity.
• The open ocean, despite covering 70% of Earth, has low productivity per area.
• Protecting high-productivity ecosystems (wetlands, coral reefs) is critical for biodiversity.

Everyday example: If you have a small garden (low productivity), you can only support a few plants. If you have a large, well-watered, fertilized garden (high productivity), you can support many plants, which can support many insects, which can support many birds. Productivity sets the stage for everything else.
principles.example: Coral reefs are sometimes called the "rainforests of the sea." They cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support about 25% of all marine species. Their high productivity comes from the symbiotic relationship between corals and algae (zooxanthellae), which capture energy from sunlight efficiently. This high productivity allows thousands of fish, invertebrates, and other species to coexist in a small area.
principles.analogy: Productivity is like the economy of an ecosystem. High productivity = strong economy with many jobs (species). Low productivity = weak economy with few jobs. Just as a strong economy can support more people, high productivity can support more species.

image.alt: A map showing NPP distribution around the world (high NPP in rainforests, low NPP in deserts). Photos of a high-productivity ecosystem (coral reef with many fish) and a low-productivity ecosystem (desert with few plants).
image.caption: Net primary productivity (NPP) limits ecosystem capacity. High NPP ecosystems (coral reefs, rainforests) support high biodiversity. Low NPP ecosystems (deserts) support fewer species.

principles.statement: Energy pyramids are always upright (wide base, narrow top) because of the laws of thermodynamics. Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it is always degraded (converted to heat) at each transfer. It is impossible for a higher trophic level to have more energy than a lower one.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• You can't have more wolves than rabbits.
• You can't have more foxes than grass.
• In any ecosystem, the total energy at higher levels is always less than at lower levels.
• Even in ecosystems where biomass pyramids appear inverted (ocean), the energy pyramid remains upright.

Everyday example: Imagine a pyramid of money. If you had $100 at the bottom, you could give $10 to the next level, $1 to the next, and so on. You could never give $200 to the next level—there's not enough energy. Energy pyramids work the same way.
principles.example: In the open ocean, the biomass of zooplankton (primary consumers) can be greater than the biomass of phytoplankton (producers) at any given moment. This seems like an inverted pyramid. But the energy pyramid is still upright because the phytoplankton reproduce so rapidly (multiple times per day) that their total energy output over time (productivity) is much higher than the zooplankton's energy. The energy flowing into the ecosystem is always greater at the producer level.
principles.analogy: Think of a company. The CEO (top predator) earns a salary. The workers (producers) earn much more in total. Even if there are fewer workers at any given moment, their total salary over the year is higher. Energy pyramids are about flow, not just standing stock.

image.alt: A diagram comparing biomass pyramids (can be inverted in oceans) with energy pyramids (always upright). Arrows showing energy flow. A graph of phytoplankton productivity vs. standing biomass.
image.caption: Energy pyramids are always upright. Even when biomass pyramids appear inverted (ocean), the energy pyramid remains wide at the base because producers have high turnover (productivity).

components.definition: Trophic levels: Producers (Level 1), Primary Consumers (Level 2), Secondary Consumers (Level 3), Tertiary Consumers (Level 4), Decomposers. Energy pyramids: energy, biomass, numbers. Productivity: GPP, NPP, secondary productivity. Ecological efficiency: 5-20% (average 10%).
components.equation:
Net Primary Productivity (NPP) = Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) - Respiration (R).
Ecological Efficiency = (Energy at level n+1) / (Energy at level n) × 100%.
Biomass = mass of organic matter per unit area (g/m²).
Energy units: kcal/m²/year or J/m²/year.
components.interpretation: NPP is the energy available to consumers. It sets the carrying capacity of the ecosystem. Higher NPP = more energy for higher trophic levels. Ecological efficiency determines how many trophic levels can be supported. If efficiency is high (20%), more levels are possible; if low (5%), fewer levels. Human appropriation of NPP (about 25%) leaves less energy for wild species, contributing to biodiversity loss.

image.alt: A summary diagram: GPP → NPP (after respiration) → primary consumers → secondary consumers → tertiary consumers. Ecological efficiency shown as 10%. A table of NPP values for different ecosystems.
image.caption: Key components of energy flow: Productivity measures energy capture; ecological efficiency measures energy transfer between trophic levels.

categories.description: Human activities are altering energy flow in ecosystems in profound ways—from reducing productivity (deforestation) to increasing it (eutrophication) to appropriating a large fraction of global NPP for human use.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Deforestation: Removing forests reduces NPP in that area. It also disrupts energy flow by removing habitat for consumers and altering local climate (less rainfall, higher temperatures).

Overfishing: Removing top predators (tuna, sharks) causes trophic cascades. Lower levels increase, then overgraze their food sources, reducing overall productivity.

Eutrophication: Excess nutrients (fertilizer runoff) increase NPP temporarily (algal blooms), but the subsequent decomposition creates dead zones where productivity drops to zero.

Climate change: Shifts NPP patterns. Some areas become more productive (higher CO₂, longer growing seasons), others less productive (drought, heat stress). Overall, climate change is reducing productivity in many regions.

Human appropriation of NPP: Humans use about 25% of global NPP for food, fiber, and fuel. This leaves less energy for wild species, contributing to the current mass extinction.

examples.process: Human Appropriation of Net Primary Productivity (HANPP)
examples.explanation:
HANPP measures how much of Earth's NPP humans use: (1) What it includes: Crops (food, animal feed, biofuels), timber, grazing land, and productivity lost to land use change (e.g., farmland replacing forest). (2) Global estimate: Humans use about 25% of global NPP (range 20-30%). (3) Implications: This energy is not available for wild species. Biodiversity loss is directly linked to HANPP—as humans take more energy, there's less for other species. (4) Regional variation: HANPP is much higher in developed regions (Europe: 50-70%) than in developing regions. (5) Future trends: As population and consumption grow, HANPP is projected to increase, further reducing energy available for wild species.

Why this matters: The 25% figure means that one-quarter of all the energy captured by photosynthesis on Earth is taken for human use. This is a major driver of the sixth mass extinction. Reducing HANPP (through efficiency, reducing food waste, shifting to plant-based diets) would leave more energy for other species.

image.alt: A pie chart showing global NPP: human use (25%), wild species (75%). A world map showing HANPP by region (highest in Europe, North America). Icons: cropland, forest, grazing land. A graph showing HANPP increasing over time.
image.caption: Human Appropriation of Net Primary Productivity (HANPP): Humans use about 25% of Earth's NPP, leaving less energy for wild species and contributing to biodiversity loss.

categories.description: Understanding energy flow helps explain the efficiency of different food choices and agricultural systems. Plant-based diets are more energy-efficient than meat-based diets because they skip the energy loss at the primary consumer level.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Energy efficiency of different food chains:
• Plant-based: Humans eat producers directly. Energy transfer: 100% (if we consider the energy in plants).
• Herbivore-based: Humans eat primary consumers. Energy transfer: 10% (plants → animals).
• Carnivore-based: Humans eat secondary consumers. Energy transfer: 1% (plants → herbivores → carnivores).
• Fish farming: Many farmed fish are carnivores (salmon). They eat fish meal from wild fish. Energy transfer: about 10-20% (wild fish → farmed fish), plus the energy to grow the wild fish.

Land use efficiency follows the same pattern. To produce 1 kg of beef, you need about 7-10 kg of grain (fed to cattle). To produce 1 kg of chicken, about 2-3 kg of grain. To produce 1 kg of grain, no animal feed is needed. This is why plant-based diets have a much lower environmental footprint.

examples.explanation:
Different diets have vastly different energy and resource requirements: (1) Meat-based diet: Requires 2-3 times more land, 3-5 times more water, and produces 2-4 times more greenhouse gases than plant-based diets. (2) Why: Because of the 10% rule. Feeding grain to animals loses 90% of the energy before the meat reaches humans. (3) Beef vs. chicken: Beef is less efficient than chicken because cattle are ruminants (they produce methane) and have lower reproduction rates. (4) Global trends: As countries develop, meat consumption increases. This increases demand for grain (animal feed), driving deforestation and land use change. (5) Sustainability: Shifting to plant-based diets or reducing meat consumption would free up land for forests and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

Why this matters: Understanding energy flow helps us make informed choices. The same land that produces 1 kg of beef could produce 10 kg of grain (in energy terms). With global population growing and climate change accelerating, the efficiency of our food system is more important than ever.

image.alt: A comparison of land use: plant-based diet (small footprint), vegetarian diet (moderate), meat-based diet (large). A graph showing greenhouse gas emissions per kg of food (beef highest, grains lowest). Icons representing different foods.
image.caption: Ecological footprint of diets: Meat-based diets require more land, water, and energy because of energy loss at each trophic level. Plant-based diets are more efficient.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Energy Flow Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Energy Flow in a Marine Ecosystem
worked_examples.problem: A marine ecosystem has the following NPP: phytoplankton produce 5,000 kcal/m²/year. Zooplankton eat the phytoplankton. Small fish eat the zooplankton. Large fish eat the small fish. (1) Assuming 10% ecological efficiency, how much energy is available at each level? (2) If a seal eats the large fish, how much energy would be available to the seal? (3) If the seal population requires 500 kcal/m²/year to survive, what area of ocean is needed to support one seal? (4) How would overfishing of large fish affect the energy available to seals?

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy at each trophic level using 10% rule.
step_by_step.calculation:
Producers (phytoplankton): 5,000 kcal/m²/year.
Primary consumers (zooplankton): 5,000 × 0.10 = 500 kcal/m²/year.
Secondary consumers (small fish): 500 × 0.10 = 50 kcal/m²/year.
Tertiary consumers (large fish): 50 × 0.10 = 5 kcal/m²/year.
Seals would be quaternary consumers (eating large fish). Energy to seals: 5 × 0.10 = 0.5 kcal/m²/year.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate area needed to support one seal.
step_by_step.calculation: If a seal needs 500 kcal/year, and each square meter provides 0.5 kcal/year, then area = 500 ÷ 0.5 = 1,000 m² (0.1 hectares). This is about the size of a small house lot.

Overfishing of large fish removes the tertiary consumer level. If large fish are removed, seals have no direct food source. They might switch to smaller fish, but that's a lower energy level (50 kcal/m²/year). To get the same 500 kcal, they would need 500 ÷ (50 × 0.10) = 100 m²? Actually, if eating small fish directly, energy to seal would be 50 × 0.10 = 5 kcal/m²/year, so area needed would be 500 ÷ 5 = 100 m²—still feasible. But if large fish are removed, the ecosystem structure changes, and small fish populations may be overfished too. The cascading effects can collapse the entire food web.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Zooplankton: 500, small fish: 50, large fish: 5 kcal/m²/year. (2) Seals: 0.5 kcal/m²/year. (3) 1,000 m² (0.1 hectares) needed to support one seal. (4) Overfishing large fish removes the main food source for seals; they may switch to smaller fish but this disrupts the ecosystem and can lead to cascading declines.
worked_examples.concept_applied: The 10% rule explains how energy decreases up trophic levels and why top predators need large territories. It also shows how removing species disrupts energy flow.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Energy from Sun to Top Predator
sections.introduction: Follow a unit of energy (1,000 kcal) from the sun, through a grassland ecosystem, as it moves through trophic levels.

process_flow.description: Sunlight strikes a field of grass. Through photosynthesis, the grass captures some of this energy and converts it to chemical energy (glucose).
process_flow.mechanism: About 1-3% of the sunlight that hits the grass is captured. The rest is reflected or passes through. The grass uses some energy for its own metabolism (respiration).
process_flow.result: Grass stores 1,000 kcal of energy as NPP

image.alt: Sun shining on grass. Diagram showing photosynthesis. Grass biomass with label: 1,000 kcal.
image.caption: Step 1: Solar energy capture. Grass stores 1,000 kcal of energy (NPP) after using some for its own metabolism.

process_flow.description: A rabbit eats the grass, obtaining some of the energy stored in the plant tissue.
process_flow.mechanism: The rabbit digests the grass. Much of the energy is used for metabolism (breathing, moving, body heat). Some is lost in feces. About 10% (100 kcal) becomes new rabbit tissue.

image.alt: Rabbit eating grass. Arrows showing energy flow: 1,000 kcal in grass, 100 kcal stored in rabbit, 900 kcal lost as heat and waste.
image.caption: Step 2: Primary consumer (rabbit) eats grass. Only about 10% of the energy (100 kcal) becomes rabbit biomass. The rest is lost.

process_flow.description: A fox eats the rabbit, obtaining the energy stored in the rabbit's tissues.
process_flow.mechanism: The fox digests the rabbit. Again, about 10% (10 kcal) becomes new fox tissue. The rest is used for metabolism, movement, and lost as heat.

image.alt: Fox eating rabbit. Arrows: 100 kcal in rabbit, 10 kcal stored in fox, 90 kcal lost.
image.caption: Step 3: Secondary consumer (fox) eats the rabbit. Only 10 kcal becomes fox biomass. Energy continues to decrease up the food chain.

process_flow.description: An eagle eats the fox, obtaining the energy stored in the fox.
process_flow.mechanism: The eagle digests the fox. About 10% (1 kcal) becomes new eagle tissue. The rest is lost as heat.

image.alt: Eagle eating fox. Arrows: 10 kcal in fox, 1 kcal stored in eagle, 9 kcal lost.
image.caption: Step 4: Top predator (eagle) eats the fox. Only 1 kcal becomes eagle biomass. After 4 levels, only 0.1% of the original energy remains.

process_flow.description: When organisms die, decomposers (bacteria, fungi) break down the dead matter.
process_flow.mechanism: Decomposers use some energy for their own metabolism. The rest is released as heat. Nutrients (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus) are released back into the soil, available for producers again.
process_flow.result: Energy lost as heat; nutrients recycled

image.alt: Dead grass, rabbit, fox, eagle being broken down by mushrooms and bacteria. Nutrients returning to soil. New grass growing.
image.caption: Step 5: Decomposers break down dead matter, recycling nutrients. Energy is lost as heat at every step, but nutrients cycle back to producers.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about energy flow—from the thermodynamics of ecosystems to global productivity patterns and the energetics of individual organisms.

process_types.energy_profile: Ecosystems obey the laws of thermodynamics. The first law (energy cannot be created or destroyed) explains why energy flows through ecosystems but is not used up. The second law (entropy increases) explains why energy is degraded to heat at each transfer.
process_types.direction: Energy enters ecosystems as sunlight (high-quality energy). It is converted to chemical energy by producers. At each transfer, some energy is lost as heat (low-quality energy). By the time energy leaves the ecosystem (as heat), it is no longer usable for work. This is why ecosystems need constant energy input.
process_types.function: Understanding thermodynamics explains why energy flow is one-way, why energy pyramids are always upright, and why productivity limits ecosystem complexity.
goals: Understand the physical laws governing energy flow
Learn why energy cannot be recycled

primary_example.explanation:
The second law of thermodynamics (entropy increases) explains why food chains are limited in length: (1) Entropy: Every energy transfer increases disorder (entropy). When a rabbit eats grass, the chemical energy in the grass is converted into rabbit tissue, heat, and waste. The energy is still there (first law), but it's less usable (second law). (2) Energy degradation: Each trophic level degrades energy further. After 4-5 transfers, the energy remaining is so dispersed (high entropy) that it can't support another trophic level. (3) Why not infinite levels: If ecosystems could support infinite trophic levels, there would be no energy loss. But because energy is always degraded, each level has less usable energy. (4) Exceptions: Ecosystems with very high productivity (coral reefs) can sometimes support 5-6 levels because the base is so large. Ecosystems with very low productivity (deserts) may only support 3 levels.

Why this matters: Thermodynamics isn't just a physics concept—it's a fundamental constraint on all life. It explains why you can't have a food chain with 20 levels, why top predators are rare, and why ecosystems need constant energy input from the sun.

image.alt: A diagram showing energy degradation: high-quality energy (sunlight) → chemical energy (plants) → heat and waste (consumers). Arrows showing increasing entropy. A graph of energy availability vs. trophic level.
image.caption: The second law of thermodynamics: Energy is degraded to heat at each trophic level. After 4-5 transfers, so little usable energy remains that another level cannot be supported.

image.alt: A diagram of an ecosystem with energy inputs and outputs. Sunlight in, heat out. Trophic levels showing energy decreasing and entropy increasing.
image.caption: Ecosystems obey the laws of thermodynamics. Energy flows in one direction, and entropy increases at each transfer.

process_types.type: Global Primary Productivity and Climate Change
process_types.energy_profile: Earth's NPP is about 100-120 billion tons of carbon per year. This productivity is not evenly distributed—it varies with climate, nutrients, and human activity. Climate change is altering NPP patterns globally.
process_types.direction: Factors affecting global NPP: (1) Temperature: Higher temperatures increase NPP up to a point, but extreme heat and drought reduce it. (2) Precipitation: NPP increases with rainfall until water is not limiting. (3) CO₂: Higher CO₂ can increase NPP (CO₂ fertilization effect), but benefits may be limited by nutrients and water. (4) Human activity: Deforestation reduces NPP; agriculture and fertilization can increase NPP locally but often at the expense of natural ecosystems.
process_types.function: Understanding global NPP patterns helps predict how climate change will affect ecosystems, food production, and the global carbon cycle.
goals: Learn about global patterns of productivity
Understand how climate change affects NPP

primary_example.explanation:
Rising atmospheric CO₂ can increase plant growth (CO₂ fertilization effect), but there are limits: (1) Mechanism: Plants use CO₂ for photosynthesis. Higher CO₂ can increase photosynthetic rates, especially in C3 plants (most trees, wheat, rice). (2) Evidence: Satellite data show that global NPP increased by about 10% from 1982-2015, partly due to CO₂ fertilization. (3) Limits: The effect is not unlimited. Plants also need water and nutrients (especially nitrogen and phosphorus). In many ecosystems, nutrient availability limits how much extra growth can occur. (4) Trade-offs: Higher CO₂ also causes warming, which can increase water stress, pests, and wildfires—all of which reduce NPP. (5) Future projections: The CO₂ fertilization effect is expected to saturate (stop increasing) as nutrients become limiting. By 2050-2100, warming and water stress may reduce NPP in many regions, especially the tropics.

Why this matters: The CO₂ fertilization effect is sometimes cited as a benefit of climate change, but it's not a solution. The effect is limited, and the negative impacts of climate change (drought, fire, pest outbreaks) are already reducing NPP in many areas. Relying on CO₂ fertilization to offset emissions is dangerous—it's not reliable and doesn't address the root problem.

image.alt: A graph showing global NPP increase (1982-2015) alongside rising CO₂. A diagram of plant growth with CO₂ and nutrients. A map showing regions where NPP is increasing (green) and decreasing (red).
image.caption: CO₂ fertilization can increase plant growth, but the effect is limited by nutrients and water. Climate change is already reducing NPP in many regions.

image.alt: A world map showing NPP distribution (high in rainforests, low in deserts). A graph of NPP by biome. A diagram of the global carbon cycle with NPP as the main flux.
image.caption: Global primary productivity varies dramatically by region. Tropical rainforests have the highest NPP; deserts and deep oceans have the lowest.

sections.relationship: Energy flow is the currency of ecosystems. It starts with the sun, captured by producers through photosynthesis. This energy then flows through trophic levels, with about 90% lost as heat at each step. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short and why there are far fewer top predators than producers. Productivity—the rate of energy capture—sets the upper limit on how much life an ecosystem can support. High-productivity ecosystems (rainforests, coral reefs) support high biodiversity; low-productivity ecosystems (deserts) support few species. Human activities are altering energy flow on a global scale—through deforestation (reducing NPP), overfishing (disrupting trophic structure), and CO₂ emissions (changing global productivity patterns). Understanding energy flow is essential for conservation, sustainable food systems, and addressing climate change. Every time you eat, you're participating in the energy flow of ecosystems—and your choices affect how much energy is left for other species.

summary:
Energy flows through ecosystems in one direction: from the sun to producers to consumers to decomposers, and finally out as heat. Unlike nutrients, energy is not recycled—it must be constantly supplied.

Trophic levels:
• Level 1: Producers (plants, algae)—capture energy from sun.
• Level 2: Primary consumers (herbivores)—eat producers.
• Level 3: Secondary consumers (carnivores)—eat primary consumers.
• Level 4: Tertiary consumers (top predators)—eat secondary consumers.
• Decomposers: Break down dead matter at all levels.

The 10% rule:
Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next level. The other 90% is lost as heat, used for metabolism, or not consumed. This explains why:
• Food chains are short (usually 4-5 levels).
• There are far more producers than top predators.
• Plant-based diets are more energy-efficient than meat-based diets.

Energy pyramids:
• Pyramid of energy: Always upright (wide base, narrow top). Shows energy decreasing at each level.
• Pyramid of biomass: Usually upright, but can be inverted in some aquatic ecosystems (because phytoplankton reproduce quickly).
• Pyramid of numbers: Often upright, but can be inverted (one tree supports many insects).

Productivity:
• Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total energy captured by producers.
• Net Primary Productivity (NPP): GPP minus respiration. NPP is the energy available to consumers.
• Secondary productivity: Energy stored by consumers (growth, reproduction).
• Global NPP: About 100-120 billion tons of carbon per year. Highest in tropical rainforests and coral reefs; lowest in deserts and deep ocean.

Trophic cascades:
Removing top predators (wolves, sharks) can cause cascading effects down the food chain. In Yellowstone, wolf reintroduction allowed willows to regrow and wetlands to recover. In oceans, overfishing sharks has led to collapses of shellfish fisheries.

Human impacts:
• Human appropriation of NPP (HANPP): Humans use about 25% of global NPP for food, fiber, and fuel. This leaves less energy for wild species.
• Eutrophication: Excess nutrients (fertilizer runoff) cause algal blooms, then dead zones.
• Deforestation: Reduces NPP and disrupts energy flow.
• Climate change: Alters global NPP patterns; CO₂ fertilization has limits, and warming reduces productivity in many regions.

Thermodynamics:
Energy flow obeys the laws of thermodynamics. The first law (energy cannot be created or destroyed) explains why energy flows through ecosystems. The second law (entropy increases) explains why energy is degraded to heat at each transfer, limiting food chain length.

Understanding energy flow—from the 10% rule to trophic cascades to global NPP patterns—is essential for ecology, conservation, and sustainable resource management. Every choice we make about food and energy affects the flow of energy through the ecosystems that sustain us.
